title: Thyroglossal Duct Cysts: Embryology, Diagnosis, and Sistrunk Procedure

questions.prompt: The thyroid descends from foramen cecum to final position during which weeks?

correct.explanation: Thyroid primordium forms at foramen cecum (week 3-4), descends through neck to pretracheal position by weeks 5-7. The thyroglossal duct is the tract of descent.
correct.mechanism: Descent path: foramen cecum→anterior to hyoid→through/around hyoid→anterior to larynx→pretracheal.

distractors.A: Too early — thyroid primordium just forming.
distractors.B: Formation begins weeks 3-4 but descent primarily weeks 5-7.
distractors.D: By weeks 10-12, descent is long complete.

correct_pathway.question: Why does the duct pass through the hyoid?
correct_pathway.expected_response: Thyroid descends anterior to developing hyoid. As hyoid ossifies, the duct becomes intimately associated with the central portion — this is why Sistrunk removes the central hyoid.

correct_pathway.question: Can you explain the underlying mechanism that supports this?
correct_pathway.expected_response: Descent path: foramen cecum→anterior to hyoid→through/around hyoid→anterior to larynx→pretracheal.

A.question: You chose 'Weeks 1-3'. Explain your reasoning.
A.expected_response: Too early — thyroid primordium just forming.

A.question: What supports 'Weeks 5-7' instead?
A.expected_response: Thyroid primordium forms at foramen cecum (week 3-4), descends through neck to pretracheal position by weeks 5-7. The thyroglossal duct is the tract of descent.

B.expected_response: Formation begins weeks 3-4 but descent primarily weeks 5-7.

B.question: What supports 'Weeks 5-7' instead?
B.expected_response: Thyroid primordium forms at foramen cecum (week 3-4), descends through neck to pretracheal position by weeks 5-7. The thyroglossal duct is the tract of descent.

D.expected_response: By weeks 10-12, descent is long complete.

D.expected_response: Thyroid primordium forms at foramen cecum (week 3-4), descends through neck to pretracheal position by weeks 5-7. The thyroglossal duct is the tract of descent.

questions.vignette: 6-year-old with midline neck mass at hyoid level.
questions.prompt: What exam finding is PATHOGNOMONIC for TGDC?

correct.explanation: Elevation with tongue protrusion is pathognomonic. The duct connects cyst to foramen cecum — tongue protrusion pulls the duct superiorly, elevating the cyst.
correct.mechanism: Tongue protrusion contracts genioglossus/hyoglossus → pulls hyoid and attached duct superiorly → cyst elevates. No other midline mass does this.

distractors.B: Many cystic masses transilluminate — not specific.
distractors.D: Swallowing movement occurs with any mass attached to laryngeal structures.

correct_pathway.question: What must you rule out before excision?
correct_pathway.expected_response: Ectopic thyroid! Must confirm normal thyroid on ultrasound. An ectopic thyroid may be the ONLY functioning thyroid tissue.

correct_pathway.question: Can you explain the underlying mechanism that supports this?
correct_pathway.expected_response: Tongue protrusion contracts genioglossus/hyoglossus → pulls hyoid and attached duct superiorly → cyst elevates. No other midline mass does this.

B.question: You chose 'Mass transilluminates'. Explain your reasoning.
B.expected_response: Many cystic masses transilluminate — not specific.

B.question: What supports 'Mass elevates with tongue protrusion' instead?
B.expected_response: Elevation with tongue protrusion is pathognomonic. The duct connects cyst to foramen cecum — tongue protrusion pulls the duct superiorly, elevating the cyst.

C.question: You chose 'Mass is non-tender'. Explain your reasoning.
What supports 'Mass elevates with tongue protrusion' instead?
C.expected_response: Elevation with tongue protrusion is pathognomonic. The duct connects cyst to foramen cecum — tongue protrusion pulls the duct superiorly, elevating the cyst.

D.question: You chose 'Mass moves with swallowing only'. Explain your reasoning.
D.expected_response: Swallowing movement occurs with any mass attached to laryngeal structures.

D.question: What supports 'Mass elevates with tongue protrusion' instead?
D.expected_response: Elevation with tongue protrusion is pathognomonic. The duct connects cyst to foramen cecum — tongue protrusion pulls the duct superiorly, elevating the cyst.

questions.prompt: Why does Sistrunk include hyoid bone removal?

options.B: Hyoid is source of cyst lining
options.D: Duct passes through central hyoid; leaving it causes ~50% recurrence

correct.explanation: Sistrunk removes cyst, central hyoid, and core of tissue to foramen cecum. Simple excision without hyoid: ~50% recurrence. Sistrunk: <5% recurrence.
correct.mechanism: The duct branches around the hyoid — removing the central hyoid captures all potential remnants.

distractors.A: Hyoid is structurally connected, not infected.
distractors.B: Cyst is lined by thyroid-type epithelium, not bone.
distractors.C: Cosmesis is not the reason — it's purely for recurrence prevention.

correct_pathway.question: What's the recurrence rate comparison?
correct_pathway.expected_response: Simple excision: ~50%. Sistrunk: <5%. This dramatic difference makes Sistrunk the standard of care.

correct_pathway.question: Can you explain the underlying mechanism that supports this?
correct_pathway.expected_response: The duct branches around the hyoid — removing the central hyoid captures all potential remnants.

A.question: You chose 'Hyoid is always infected'. Explain your reasoning.
A.expected_response: Hyoid is structurally connected, not infected.

A.question: What supports 'Duct passes through central hyoid; leaving it causes ~50% recurrence' instead?
A.expected_response: Sistrunk removes cyst, central hyoid, and core of tissue to foramen cecum. Simple excision without hyoid: ~50% recurrence. Sistrunk: <5% recurrence.

B.question: You chose 'Hyoid is source of cyst lining'. Explain your reasoning.
B.expected_response: Cyst is lined by thyroid-type epithelium, not bone.

B.question: What supports 'Duct passes through central hyoid; leaving it causes ~50% recurrence' instead?
B.expected_response: Sistrunk removes cyst, central hyoid, and core of tissue to foramen cecum. Simple excision without hyoid: ~50% recurrence. Sistrunk: <5% recurrence.

C.question: You chose 'Improves cosmetic outcome'. Explain your reasoning.
C.expected_response: Cosmesis is not the reason — it's purely for recurrence prevention.

C.question: What supports 'Duct passes through central hyoid; leaving it causes ~50% recurrence' instead?
C.expected_response: Sistrunk removes cyst, central hyoid, and core of tissue to foramen cecum. Simple excision without hyoid: ~50% recurrence. Sistrunk: <5% recurrence.

options.B: Normal thyroid in expected pretracheal position

correct.explanation: Must confirm normal thyroid is present. ~1-2% of TGDC patients have ectopic thyroid as ONLY thyroid tissue. Excising without checking = permanent hypothyroidism.
correct.mechanism: Thyroid can arrest anywhere along descent path. Ectopic thyroid in the duct may be the only functioning tissue.

distractors.A: Size helps planning but isn't the safety issue.
distractors.C: Calcifications might suggest rare carcinoma but aren't the priority.
distractors.D: Compartment number helps differential but safety is confirming thyroid presence.

correct_pathway.question: What if no thyroid seen in normal position?
correct_pathway.expected_response: DO NOT EXCISE. Order TSH/free T4 and nuclear medicine thyroid scan. If mass contains functioning thyroid tissue, the patient needs endocrinology, not surgery.

correct_pathway.question: Can you explain the underlying mechanism that supports this?
correct_pathway.expected_response: Thyroid can arrest anywhere along descent path. Ectopic thyroid in the duct may be the only functioning tissue.

A.expected_response: Size helps planning but isn't the safety issue.

A.question: What supports 'Normal thyroid in expected pretracheal position' instead?
A.expected_response: Must confirm normal thyroid is present. ~1-2% of TGDC patients have ectopic thyroid as ONLY thyroid tissue. Excising without checking = permanent hypothyroidism.

C.question: You chose 'No calcifications'. Explain your reasoning.
C.expected_response: Calcifications might suggest rare carcinoma but aren't the priority.

C.question: What supports 'Normal thyroid in expected pretracheal position' instead?
C.expected_response: Must confirm normal thyroid is present. ~1-2% of TGDC patients have ectopic thyroid as ONLY thyroid tissue. Excising without checking = permanent hypothyroidism.

D.question: You chose 'Number of compartments'. Explain your reasoning.
D.expected_response: Compartment number helps differential but safety is confirming thyroid presence.

D.expected_response: Must confirm normal thyroid is present. ~1-2% of TGDC patients have ectopic thyroid as ONLY thyroid tissue. Excising without checking = permanent hypothyroidism.

questions.vignette: Pathology reveals papillary thyroid carcinoma in excised TGDC.

options.C: Thyroid ultrasound to evaluate for concurrent thyroid carcinoma

correct.explanation: TGDC carcinoma (~1% of cases) requires thyroid ultrasound because 25-30% have concurrent thyroid cancer. If thyroid normal, Sistrunk may suffice. If thyroid nodules found, thyroidectomy indicated.
correct.mechanism: Same molecular pathways (BRAF, RET/PTC) drive both TGDC and primary thyroid papillary carcinoma.

distractors.A: Observation without thyroid evaluation insufficient — 25-30% have concurrent thyroid cancer.
distractors.B: Radiation not first-line for papillary carcinoma.
distractors.D: Thyroidectomy premature without first evaluating the thyroid gland.

correct_pathway.question: What predicts good prognosis?
correct_pathway.expected_response: Small (<2cm), papillary type, negative margins, no concurrent thyroid cancer = excellent prognosis with Sistrunk alone.

correct_pathway.question: Can you explain the underlying mechanism that supports this?
correct_pathway.expected_response: Same molecular pathways (BRAF, RET/PTC) drive both TGDC and primary thyroid papillary carcinoma.

A.question: You chose 'Observation only'. Explain your reasoning.
A.expected_response: Observation without thyroid evaluation insufficient — 25-30% have concurrent thyroid cancer.

A.question: What supports 'Thyroid ultrasound to evaluate for concurrent thyroid carcinoma' instead?
A.expected_response: TGDC carcinoma (~1% of cases) requires thyroid ultrasound because 25-30% have concurrent thyroid cancer. If thyroid normal, Sistrunk may suffice. If thyroid nodules found, thyroidectomy indicated.

B.question: You chose 'Radiation therapy'. Explain your reasoning.
B.expected_response: Radiation not first-line for papillary carcinoma.

B.question: What supports 'Thyroid ultrasound to evaluate for concurrent thyroid carcinoma' instead?
B.expected_response: TGDC carcinoma (~1% of cases) requires thyroid ultrasound because 25-30% have concurrent thyroid cancer. If thyroid normal, Sistrunk may suffice. If thyroid nodules found, thyroidectomy indicated.

D.question: You chose 'Immediate total thyroidectomy'. Explain your reasoning.
D.expected_response: Thyroidectomy premature without first evaluating the thyroid gland.

D.question: What supports 'Thyroid ultrasound to evaluate for concurrent thyroid carcinoma' instead?
D.expected_response: TGDC carcinoma (~1% of cases) requires thyroid ultrasound because 25-30% have concurrent thyroid cancer. If thyroid normal, Sistrunk may suffice. If thyroid nodules found, thyroidectomy indicated.

questions.vignette: Parent asks why the mass is in the middle of the neck.

options.A: At or near the hyoid bone (~60-65%)

correct.explanation: ~60-65% present at hyoid level. ~20% suprahyoid, ~15-20% infrahyoid. The hyoid is where the duct is most constrained.
correct.mechanism: The thyroglossal duct's path passes through/around the hyoid — the narrowest passage point where remnants most commonly form.

distractors.B: Thyroid cartilage level is infrahyoid — only ~15-20%.
distractors.C: Submental TGDCs are rare; dermoid cysts more common there.

correct_pathway.question: Differential for midline neck mass by location?
correct_pathway.expected_response: Hyoid: TGDC, dermoid. Submental: dermoid, ranula. Suprahyoid: TGDC, lingual thyroid. Thyroid level: thyroid nodule, infrahyoid TGDC. Tongue protrusion test distinguishes TGDC from all others.

correct_pathway.question: Can you explain the underlying mechanism that supports this?
correct_pathway.expected_response: The thyroglossal duct's path passes through/around the hyoid — the narrowest passage point where remnants most commonly form.

B.question: You chose 'Thyroid cartilage level'. Explain your reasoning.
B.expected_response: Thyroid cartilage level is infrahyoid — only ~15-20%.

B.question: What supports 'At or near the hyoid bone (~60-65%)' instead?
B.expected_response: ~60-65% present at hyoid level. ~20% suprahyoid, ~15-20% infrahyoid. The hyoid is where the duct is most constrained.

C.expected_response: Submental TGDCs are rare; dermoid cysts more common there.
